title: Citrix Anomalous Session Behavior (Off-Hours, Unusual Apps)
dataSources: `index=xd` `sourcetype="citrix:broker:session"` and `sourcetype="citrix:broker:app_usage"` (app title, `session_start`, `session_end`, `user`, `day_of_week`, `client_ip`); optional baseline from summary indexing
app: Citrix Monitor Service or broker-derived usage via OData collectors, custom forwarders, optional Splunk User Behavior Analytics
spl:
index=xd (sourcetype="citrix:broker:session" OR sourcetype="citrix:broker:app_usage") earliest=-7d
| eval hr=tonumber(strftime(_time, "%H")), wday=tonumber(strftime(_time, "%w")), du=tonumber(session_duration_sec)
| eval off=if(hr<5 OR hr>22 OR wday==0 OR wday==6,1,0)
| eventstats median(du) as medu by app_name
| eval longsess=if(du>(3*coalesce(medu,0)) AND coalesce(medu,0)>0,1,0)
| where off=1 OR longsess=1
| bin _time span=1d
| stats count as ev, dc(app_name) as appc, max(longsess) as has_long by _time, user
| where ev>5 OR appc>15
| table _time, user, ev, appc, has_long
description: Baseline user behavior in virtual apps: typical hours, common titles, and session length. Off-hours or weekend spikes and unusual first-time applications (especially data-heavy or admin tools) can signal account compromise, side work by insiders, or automation errors. This use case combines time-of-day signals with per-app duration compared to a rolling median to reduce noise for routine shift work.
value: Baseline user behavior in virtual apps: typical hours, common titles, and session length. Off-hours or weekend spikes and unusual first-time applications (especially data-heavy or admin tools) can signal account compromise, side work by insiders, or automation errors. This use case combines time-of-day signals with per-app duration compared to a rolling median to reduce noise for routine shift work.
implementation: Feed shift schedules from HR or facilities into a lookup to refine off-hours. Maintain an allow list for on-call and batch accounts. `eventstats` medians are a starting point; consider ML assistants or user-level baselines for large estates. Triage with the impossible-travel and privilege use cases. Respect privacy: aggregate before HR notification policies.
visualization: Heatmap: events by user hour; table: top users for off-hours; scatter: `session_duration_sec` by app (spot long tails).

references.title: Citrix — Monitor and Director session analytics

knownFalsePositives: Global BPO, weekend SRE, and batch job operators naturally use 'off hours' and niche published apps. OU- or country-based baselines, holiday and on-call calendars, and a minimum deviation from a 30-day user habit reduce one-off 'weird' from critical insider.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• `index=xd` with `session`+`app_usage` including `app_name` and duration (or `session_end`-`start`). `lookups/shift_by_site` for off-hours, `lookups/allow_accounts` (batch/on-call) to drop noise.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
If duration absent: `eval` from epoch fields, guard nulls. `SEDCMD-` to normalize `app_name` (strip publisher). Optional summary index: nightly per-user `dc(app)` and median `du` to seed baselines. Respect privacy: aggregate before HR steps.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
`off` hours per region: start 22–5 local; add weekend flag. `eventstats` medians need enough volume per app—exclude rare apps in subsearch. Pilot one BU: tune `ev>5` and `appc>15` from baseline; raise if shift FP. Throttle: user+1d.

Step 3 — Validate
One-week Director app usage top-line vs Splunk `dc(app_name)` for sample users. Manually recompute one app’s median `du` vs `eventstats` to verify.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Heatmap: hour x user. Escalation: L2 for off-hours+longsess on `sensitive_apps` lookup; L3+HR only per policy. Run after impossible-travel. Quarterly: FP review; union with UBA. Document lawful monitoring approval.
grandmaExplanation: We learn what a normal work pattern looks like in your app farm, then flag the odd late nights, odd weekends, and strangely long stints in unusual tools — so you see odd use before a loss happens.
